draft.introduction: The Instruction of Ptahhotep (c. 2350 BCE) is the oldest complete wisdom text in world literature. Attributed to a vizier of the Fifth Dynasty, it presents thirty-seven maxims on justice, humility, and proper conduct in hierarchical society.

translation.text: Do not be proud on account of your knowledge. Consult with the ignorant as well as the wise, for the limits of skill have not been reached, and there is no craftsman who has fully acquired his mastery. Good speech is more hidden than malachite, yet it may be found among the women at the millstones.
translation.original_text: m ḏd ib=k ḥr rḫ=k. nḏ.w ḥna ḫmm jwā.w m-mjt.t ḥna rḫ.w. n spr dr.w āḫm.w

translator_notes.note: The opening maxim’s insistence on consulting even the ignorant because “the limits of skill have not been reached” is remarkably Socratic in spirit, predating Socrates by nearly two millennia. The observation that wisdom can be found “among the women at the millstones” is unusual for its acknowledgment of non-elite knowledge.
